text: Just when I think you couldn't possibly be any dumber you go and do something like this. >> I, PARKED, THE, CAR. >> LET'S, HAVE, NO, ONE, DO, THAT, AGAIN. >> Animal, Farm.

>> Let, us, discuss, the, concept, of, death, of the author, shall we, dear viewer? Based on the 1960s essay by the French literary critic Roland Barthe, it suggests that when it comes to assessing a piece of media, the original intent and ideas of the creator are less relevant than the individual perceptions and experiences of the audience. >> All, right,, guy. >> Basically,, it, doesn't, matter, what, the creator of the work tried to say with it once it's released into the world. All

that really matters is what you personally take from it. >> That, doesn't, make, sense., And, by, golly,, I do believe that Andy Circus is pushing that concept to its limits with his very unique take on Animal Farm, which uh reimagines George Orwell's satirical critique of totalitarian communism and Stalin's cult of personality as a wacky contemporary animated comedy starring Seth Rogan, where the real enemy is a tech billionaire who happens to drive a cyber truck. >> Right.

You know, I can't be 100% sure with this one, but I'm pretty confident we live in the worst timeline imaginable. Look, I'm not against the concept of adapting classic stories to fit with contemporary events as long as they stay true to the original themes and ideas. Like Apocalypse Now took Joseph Conrad's novel Heart of Darkness, which was originally set in 19th century Africa and transplanted it into 1960s Vietnam. It worked in that case because both stories dealt with the same basic ideas

the dehumanizing and corrupting nature of power, the loss of morality when the civilization that underpins it vanishes the dangers of imperialism and colonialism. But what happens when you specifically set out to change the very core of the story to deliver a completely different message than the one intended by the author? Well, you end up with Animal Farm, I guess. But why does it really matter? Well, it matters because Animal Farm just so happens to be one of the most iconic and

widely reference political satires ever written. A scathing critique of totalitarian communism and Stalin's cult of personality, and a pretty biting observation about human nature, our greed, our lust for power, and our willingness to compromise our moral principles for personal gain. In short it's not really the sort of thing that lends itself to a goofy Seth Rogan kids comedy whose main message seems to be rich people bad. Maybe you should lead by example, Seth, and donate all that
money you've got in the bank.

Now, you might find this hard to believe nowadays, but once upon a time, I actually used to read books, and George Orwell's works made quite the impression on teenage me. So, I have to admit I do have a certain attachment to Animal Farm. The original novella roughly follows the events of the Russian Revolution and the eventual rise of Stalin as sole dictator and the purges and totalitarianism that came with him. The animals of Manor Farm stage a rebellion against their corrupt and

incompetent human overlords, i.e. the Russian aristocracy and usher in a new society based on the promise of equality and freedom for all as imagined by old major. basically a combination of Marx and Lenin who dies before he can see his dream accomplished. In the power vacuum that follows, a pair of pigs, Napoleon and Snowball, Stalin and Trosky, fight for control of the farm until Snowball eventually gets pushed out and exiled and Napoleon becomes the supreme leader.

The more time that passes, the more his rule devolves into a dictatorship as he purges the farm of his enemies, real or imagined, and keeps the population in perpetual fear and poverty despite assuring them that they're better off than they were before. Eventually, he and the other pigs become so human in their behavior that they end up negotiating and trading with actual humans from other farms, walking around on two legs and hosting lavish parties while the rest of the animals watch from

outsides. With all their greed and their lies and their lust for power, they've become the very thing they swore to overthrow. I mean, it's not exactly subtle, but in case you still don't get it, Orwell himself straight up explained multiple times what his intent was with Animal Farm. It was a straightup critique of Stalinism and the authoritarianist dictatorships of the time. But more than that, it was a critique of the political ideals that brought them into existence in the first

place. No matter how noble your intentions and no matter how much you might promise freedom and equality, a new class of rulers and aristocrats is always going to rise up just with different names. The cycle of revolution, corruption, and exploitation keeps repeating because fundamentally humans are not equal, and you can't force them to be. There's always going to be some [ __ ] who wants more. more money, more resources, more power, and he's willing to break, exploit, or just

straight up rewrite the rules to get what he wants. It's all very interesting stuff. The obvious problem these days though, is twofold. One, the satire is kind of lost because most people now don't even have a functioning understanding of what the Russian Revolution was, never mind the finer details, the timeline of events, or the major players involved. And two, the idea of a Hollywood movie that suggests there might be a few problems with this whole socialism thing is a bit like

striding into the Vatican in rainbow colored underwear and saying, "Hey chaps, this whole religion thing is a bit silly, isn't it? It's clearly never going to happen." And so we end up with this thing instead, where the real villain of the piece is a thinly veiled standin for Elon Musk. And the corruption of the animal farm utopia doesn't come about because of personal ambition, paranoia, and the flawed nature of the human condition, but because big houses and sports cars. Am I

right? Am I so out of touch? No, it's the children who are wrong. >> And, the, thing, I, can't, help, but, wondering is why? Don't get me wrong, there's definitely plenty of things to criticize about modern-day crony capitalism worker exploitation, and corporate monopolies, but Animal Farm isn't the framework to do it in. Fundamentally you're trying to tell the wrong story in the wrong setting, and it would have been way easier and probably less intellectually offensive to just come up

with a whole new narrative that better suits your objectives. I honestly can't believe that Andy Circus is directing this. I can't believe he's been working on this project for 15 years and this is what he produced. I can't believe Seth Rogan is still getting hired for movies when his films are usually the place where fun, common sense, and good taste go to die. In short, I think that remaking Animal Farm for modern audiences might have been a bad idea. Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
